title: Bailout Stigma
abstract: We develop a model of bailout stigma in which accepting a bailout signals a firm's balance-sheet weakness and reduces its funding prospects. To avoid stigma, high-quality firms withdraw from subsequent financing after receiving bailouts or refuse bailouts altogether to send a favorable signal. The former leads to a short-lived stimulation followed by a market freeze even worse than if there were no bailout. The latter revives the funding market, albeit with delay, to the level achievable without any stigma and implements a constrained optimal outcome. A menu of multiple bailout programs compounds bailout stigma and exacerbates the market freeze.

article.introduction:
History is fraught with financialcrises and large-scale government interventions, the latter often involving a highly visible and significant wealth transfer from taxpayers to banks and their creditors. As a recent example, during the 2007 to 2009 Great Recession, the U.S. government paid $125 billion for assets worth $86 billion to $109 billion to the nine largest banks under the Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) (Veronesi and Zingales (2010)). A rationale for such public interventions—to be called bailouts throughout the paper—is that the government can jump-start a market that would otherwise freeze due to adverse selection. By cleaning up bad assets, or “dregs skimming,” through public bailouts, the government can improve market confidence and galvanize transactions in healthier assets (Philippon and Skreta (2012), Tirole (2012)). However, the flip side of such dregs skimming is that bailouts can stigmatize their recipients, increasing future borrowing costs. The fear of such stigma may in turn discourage financially distressed firms from accepting bailout offers in the first place, undermining the effectiveness of such interventions.1,$^{,}$2
  In this paper, we study a dynamic model of bailouts to understand how reputational concerns impact the effectiveness of public bailouts and how an optimal bailout policy should be designed in light of these concerns. To address these questions, we present a two-period model of a bailout program in which the government offers to purchase assets from firms. The focus on asset purchase as the bailout tool allows us to capture firms' reputational concerns in a simple and parsimonious way. There is a continuum of firms, each with one unit of a legacy asset in each period. Asset quality in both periods is identical for each firm and is the firm's private information. In each period, firms have access to profitable investment opportunities. However, liquidity constraints and the nonpledgeability of projects imply that firms need to sell assets to fund those projects. As in Tirole (2012), we focus on the case in which adverse selection leads to a market freeze and in turn to calls for a public bailout. To understand the reputational consequence of accepting a bailout, we assume that the government runs a bailout program in the first period only. The sale of assets in the market is not publicly observed, while firms' acceptance of the bailout offer is observed. The market updates its belief about the quality of assets based on the observation of firms' decisions in the first period, and thus makes its second-period offer accordingly.
  Bailout stigma is captured in our model by the unfavorable terms that bailout recipients suffer from the sale of their assets in the second period. As Philippon and Skreta (2012) and Tirole (2012) show, a key function of a public bailout is dregs skimming: by taking out the left tail of the worst-quality assets, a bailout improves the perceived quality of remaining assets, and thus rejuvenates asset trade in the market. But this means that the market will believe that those firms that accept the bailout have worse assets than those that refuse the bailout. Such a belief is reflected in the differential treatment of the two groups of firms in the second period: the offer the market makes to bailout recipients would generally be worse than the offer made to bailout holdouts.
  The precise mechanism through which the bailout stigma impacts the efficacy of the bailout depends crucially on firms' strategic responses to the bailout offer, as manifested in two possible equilibrium outcomes—short-lived stimulation and delayed stimulation.
  A short-lived stimulation equilibrium arises when high-quality firms strategically avoid bailout stigma by accepting a bailout in the first period but withdrawing from the market in the second period. Suppose that high-quality firms behave in this way. Then, the market in the second period would believe that the bailout recipients thatdoparticipate in the second period have bad assets and therefore assign stigma to them. To avoid that stigma, firms would rather sell their assets to the market at a discount in the first period. This further deteriorates their sale terms, ultimately inflicting a commensurate haircut on the market sellers; in effect, bailout stigma “spreads” from bailout recipients to market sellers in this equilibrium. This contagion of stigma leads firms with high-quality assets to accept a bailout offer but withdraw from the second-period market to avoid that stigma, thus validating the initial hypothesis.3
  The presence of high-quality firms that withdraw from the subsequent market/financing undermines the overall efficacy of the bailout, as their withdrawal exacerbates the stigma for those participating in the subsequent market, which has a chilling effect on the asset markets in both periods. Unlike Tirole (2012), in which the government intervention boosts confidence in private markets, intervention now undermines market confidence. The consequence is devastating:the market freeze is even worse than if there were no bailout!However, this does not mean that a bailout has no beneficial effect. The increased investment by bailout recipients in the first period may outweigh the dampening effect on the markets. Nevertheless, stimulation is short-lived in this equilibrium. We show that the policymaker can avoid this equilibrium by offering sufficiently generous bailout terms—in particular, high purchase prices for assets, in which case stigma will take a different form: delayed stimulation.
  A delayed stimulation equilibrium arises when high-quality firms refuse to sell to either the government or to the market in the first period. They do so to establish a good reputation in order to sell their assets in the second period at favorable terms. This equilibrium is possible when the bailout offer is generous enough to attract a large fraction of firms with low-quality assets. This allows firms to send a credible signal about their asset quality when they refuse the bailout offer. Buyers then respond with an attractive offer in the second period, one that makes it worthwhile for high-quality firms to forgo asset sale in the first period. In sum, the equilibrium endogenously creates an opportunity for high-quality firms to signal their financial strength by rejecting the government's generous offer.
  The favorable signaling opportunity offsets the adverse effect of bailout stigma, even though market rejuvenation is delayed to the second period. The presence of firms rejecting a bailout means that the volume of asset trade in the first period is lower than that of the one-period benchmark with the same bailout offer. In an extreme case, it is even possible that the bailout has no stimulation effect in the first period relative to the laissez-faire economy. Such an initial lack of response may be seen as a policy failure. However, the policy “quietly” strengthens market confidence in the asset quality of refusing firms, which bears dividends in the second period. In fact, overall trade volume is higher than that in a short-lived stimulation equilibrium; remarkably, it is the sameas if there were no bailout stigma, that is, if the identities of bailout recipients were concealed, which is generally difficult to achieve in practice. Strikingly, a bailout offer is more effective when many firms reject it (to build a favorable reputation) rather than accept it. Rejection of bailouts could therefore be a blessing in disguise.
  The desirability of the delayed stimulation equilibrium is further reinforced when the costs of bailouts are taken into account. When such social costs are included in the model, we show that a delayed stimulation equilibrium (as well as a secret bailout) induced by a suitable bailout offer is constrained optimal among all bailout mechanisms offered in the first period, including those involving a menu of multiple bailout terms. By contrast, a short-lived stimulation equilibrium is strictly suboptimal regardless of the bailout terms. This is because the severe stigma arising from that equilibrium requires a high deficit, and a correspondingly high social cost, to generate the same degree of trade stimulation. To eliminate the possibility of this less desirable equilibrium, the policymaker may wish to make the terms of bailout even more generous than would be optimal. This approach, although departing from the classical Bagehot rule,4is consistent with the approach taken by policymakers during the Great Recession. Finally, the optimality of a single bailout program calls into question the wisdom of offering a menu of bailout programs, which can never be socially desirable. Multiple programs give firms increased opportunities and incentives for signaling, which compounds the overall stigma and market freeze.
  The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIpresents our model while SectionIIanalyses several benchmark cases. In SectionIII, we study various equilibria under government intervention. SectionIVprovides a welfare analysis of bailout policy. SectionVdiscusses related literature. SectionVIconcludes. Proofs not contained in the main text are deferred to theAppendixand theInternet Appendix.5
article.I. Model:
We adopt a model that extends Tirole (2012) to a setup that admits bailout stigma. There is a continuum of firms each endowed with two units of legacy assets with the same value; one unit of the asset becomes available in each of two periods (t=1,2$t = 1, 2$) for possible sale.6The asset valueθ$\theta$of each firm is privately known to that firm and distributed on [0,1] according to cumulative distribution function (cdf)F$F$with densityf$f$. For convenience, we hereafter call a firm with legacy assetθ$\theta$a type-θ$\theta$firm. Throughout, we assume thatf$f$is log-concave, that is,d2logf(θ)/dθ2<0$d^2 \log f(\theta)/d \theta ^2<0$. Log-concavity off$f$implies intuitive properties that we will use on truncated conditional expectation: for any0<a<b<1$0<a<b<1$, we have0<∂∂aE[θ|a≤θ≤b]$0 < \frac{\partial }{\partial a} \mathbb {E}[\theta | a \le \theta \le b]$and∂∂bE[θ|a≤θ≤b]<1$\frac{ \partial }{\partial b} \mathbb {E}[\theta | a \le \theta \le b] < 1$(see Bagnoli and Bergstrom (2005)). We additionally assume that for eachb∈(0,1]$b\in (0,1]$,2E[θ|a<θ<b]−E[θ|θ≤a]$2\mathbb {E}[\theta | a < \theta < b] - \mathbb {E}[ \theta | \theta \le a]$is increasing ina$a$for anya∈(0,b)$a\in (0,b)$. These properties, which hold for many well-known distributions, facilitate the characterization of our equilibria.
  In each period, an investment project becomes available to each firm. The project is socially valuable withnetreturnS>0$S>0$but requires funding ofI>0$I>0$. A firm can finance the project by selling its legacy asset each period. As we will see, the outcome from this laissez-faire regime will typically be inefficient due to the adverse selection associated with uncertain asset value. This inefficiency rationalizes a government bailout in the form of an offer to purchase legacy assets at some pricepg$p_g$. The government purchase pricepg$p_g$is initially exogenous (at a level aboveI$I$); in SectionIV, we later discuss how it may be chosen optimally in light of the public cost of a bailout. The timeline of our full game is depicted in Figure1.
  To focus our attention on the main object of interest, namely, bailout stigma, we make several simplifying assumptions. First, just like Tirole (2012), we assume that the limited pledgeability of the project inhibits direct financing. This means that the sale of legacy assets is a firm's only means of funding the project. In the same vein, we consider the government's purchase of legacy assets as the only means of government bailout.7This is primarily a simplifying assumption. As shown by Philippon and Skreta (2012), the main thrust of the analysis extends to the case in which the project can be pledged along with legacy assets as collateral to obtain financing.8From this broader perspective, adverse selection with respect to legacy assets must be interpreted as corresponding to their values as collateral required for financing; our results can be translated into this broader context naturally.
  Second, we assume that the return from thet=1$t = 1$project is not pledgeable and does not accrue until each firm needs to fund itst=2$t = 2$project. This implies that the firm is not able to use the return from thet=1$t=1$project to finance thet=2$t = 2$project.
  Third, we assume that each firm discounts itst=2$t = 2$payoff with relative weightδ<1$\delta < 1$.9This assumption is introduced primarily to rationalize the equilibrium structure in our analysis (see Lemma1) by invoking the single-crossing property of firms' preferences, so throughout we restrict our analysis to the limiting case ofδ$\delta$approaching one.
  Fourth, as is standard in the literature, we assume that asset buyers in the market are competitive and make purchase offers in Bertrand fashion. Specifically, we require that an equilibrium admit no deviation offer that a positive measure of firms have strict incentives to accept and that at least breaks even for the deviating buyer.10This means that in equilibrium, all buyers must break even, since otherwise there will exist a weakly profitable deviating offer that will be attractive for a positive measure of firms. Buyers live for one period and make offers that would break even in expectation. Importantly, buyers int=2$t = 2$can make rational inference about firms' types from their observable behavior int=1$t=1$, in particular with their acceptance/rejection of a bailout offer.
  Fifth, we assume that the sale of assets to the market int=1$t = 1$is private and therefore not revealed to buyers int=2$t=2$. This implies that buyers in thet=2$t=2$market cannot distinguish between those that sold in thet=1$t=1$market and those that did not. Again the primary reason for this assumption is to simplify the analysis by shutting off channels of dynamic information revelation. However, this assumption is well justified given that many important financial and real assets are sold privately over the counter. The main thrust of our results extends to the case of observable sale, as shown by the working paper version of our paper (see Che, Choe, and Rhee (2018)). Moreover, this assumption makes a comparison with Tirole (2012) transparent, which helps isolate the effect of stigma.
  Sixth, we assume that a government bailout is available only int=1$t=1$. This is consistent with observed practice—governments refrain from engaging in long-term bailouts and from complete “nationalization” of distressed firms (which would be equivalent to purchasing two units of the asset in our model). Our goal is to study the reputational consequence of accepting or rejecting a bailout, which can be studied most effectively when no bailout is available in the second period.11
  Finally, we assume “transparent” bailouts, that is, a setting in which thet=2$t=2$market observes the identities of firms that have accepted bailouts. Transparent bailouts not only highlight the stigma effect most clearly, but also are important from a practical perspective. While secret bailouts may address the stigma problem, secrecy is often difficult to achieve in practice. Nevertheless, one may consider suppressing information about bailouts at least in part in the spirit of information design (Bergemann and Morris (2019)). Our analysis in SectionIVadopts this broad information design perspective and shows that a transparent bailout is without loss of generality given the selection of equilibrium.

By offering a higher pricepg$p_g$than the laissez-faire pricep0$p_0$, the government does indeed remove relatively low-value assets from the market, which improves the perceived value of those assets sold to the market and hence alleviates adverse selection.
  More importantly for our purpose, assets are sold to the government at the same price as they are sold to the market. This reflects the absence of stigma associated with accepting a bailout. Simply put, in the one-period problem, firms that accept the bailout can do so without fear of consequences, since the game ends immediately after the bailout.
  Remark 1. (The role of the equilibrium refinement in Tirole ([2012]))To obtain the dregs-skimming role of bailout, Tirole (2012) invokes an equilibrium refinement, whereby the market sale collapses with an arbitrarily small probability.   To identify the effects of bailout stigma, it is helpful to understand what would happen if the policymaker could eliminate the stigma altogether. Imagine that the policymaker were able to completely and successfully conceal the identities of firms that accept the government offer. This kind of secrecy would be an important feature of bailout and is worth studying in its own right, precisely because of the issue of stigma.19But here, we view secrecy primarily as a benchmark against which transparent bailouts are compared, given our premise that complete secrecy is difficult to achieve in practice.20

Since, under secrecy, neither sales to the market nor sales to the government are observed (the former by assumption and the latter by secrecy), firms need not worry about the signaling consequences of theirt=1$t=1$actions. article.III. Government Bailout and Stigma:
We now turn to the two-period game depicted in Figure1. We continue to assume that the government offer is above the laissez-faire price:pg>p0$p_g > p_0$. Otherwise, there is only a trivial equilibrium in which the laissez-faire outcome prevails, with no firms accepting the government offer.
  We begin by analyzing the structure of a possible equilibrium.
  Lemma 1.In any equilibrium withpg>p0$p_g>p_0$, there are three cutoffs0<θ̂≤θ̂g≤θ2$0<\hat{\theta }\le \hat{\theta }_g\le \theta _2$such that typesθ≤θ̂$\theta \le \hat{\theta }$sell assets in both periods, some measure of whom sell to the government and the remainder of whom sell to the market int=1$t=1$; typesθ∈(θ̂,θ̂g]$\theta \in (\hat{\theta }, \hat{\theta }_g]$sell only to the government int=1$t=1$but do not sell int=2$t=2$; typesθ∈(θ̂g,θ2]$\theta \in (\hat{\theta }_g,\theta _2]$sell only int=2$t=2$; and typesθ>θ2$\theta >\theta _2$never sell their assets in either period.
  
  The structure of an equilibrium is depicted in Figure6.
  Lemma1rests on several observations. First, firms' preferences satisfy the single-crossing property, implying that a lower type has greater incentives to sell than a higher type in either period. This implies that the total number of units sold in equilibrium across the two periods must be nonincreasing inθ$\theta$. Second, the fact that buyers (either the government or the market) never ration sellers means that the quantity traded for each firm must be either zero or one in each period.21Third, an arbitrarily small discounting of the second-period payoff, along with the first two observations, implies that, among those firms that sell only in one period, early sellers are of lower types than late sellers.22These observations give rise to the stated cutoff structure, as depicted in Figure6. We omit the formal proof since it follows from a standard argument based on these observations.
  In what follows, we limit attention to the casepg<1−S$p_g<1-S$, that is, the case in which the offer is not so high that all firms would accept the bailout.23Lemma1then implies that there are only two possible types of equilibria: (i) short-lived stimulation equilibria (SSE) and (ii) delayed stimulation equilibria (DSE), depending on whether the stimulation effect of a bailout arises int=1$t=1$or is delayed tot=2$t=2$. More formally, these two types of equilibria correspond to the cases whereθ̂g=θ2$\hat{\theta }_g =\theta _2$andθ̂g<θ2$\hat{\theta }_g<\theta _2$, respectively, in the cutoff structure characterized in Lemma1. In SectionIof theInternet Appendix, we formally show that these are the only possible types of equilibria.
  The SSE correspond to the caseθ̂g=θ2$\hat{\theta }_g=\theta _2$in Lemma1, and are depicted in Figure7.   We highlight three features of SSE. First, bailout recipients suffer stigma. In thet=2$t=2$market, the bailout recipients that appear in that market are believed to be of typeθ¯g$\overline{\theta }_g$(on average), while those that sold to the market int=1$t=1$are believed to be of typeθ¯m>θ¯g$\overline{\theta }_m > \overline{\theta }_g$.24Thus, assets held by bailout recipients are sold at a discount equal toΔ=θ¯m−θ¯g$\Delta =\overline{\theta }_m-\overline{\theta }_g$since the market correctly infers the difference between their average asset values. Of course, this stigma must be compensated int=1$t=1$, or else no firm would accept the bailout. In particular, the government must pay more than the market does for the asset int=1$t=1$. Since the government offer is fixed atpg$p_g$, this means that the market int=1$t=1$must clear at pricepg−Δ$p_g-\Delta$. In other words, buyers demand a haircutΔ$\Delta$from firms selling to them for avoiding that stigma. The stigma therefore leads to a commensurate markdown of price for assets traded int=1$t=1$market.25Since the market is competitive, buyers cannot earn positive profits, so this simply means that the average typeθ¯m$\overline{\theta }_m$of firms selling to the market must (endogenously) equalpg−Δ$p_g-\Delta$.
  Second, dregs skimming by government bailout—featured prominently in Tirole's model—does not occur here. More specifically, there is a positive measure of firmsθ∈(θ̂,θ̂g]$\theta \in (\hat{\theta }, \hat{\theta }_g]$that accept the government offer. The assets sold by these firms have strictly higher value thanθ̂$\hat{\theta }$, the highest value of assets sold to the market. This contrasts with the equilibrium in Tirole (2012), in which the assets sold to the government are worth strictly less than those sold to the private markets. Bailout stigma here creates the incentive for high-type firms to mitigate or avoid the stigma altogether. Selling to the market int=1$t=1$instead is one option, but doing so is subject to a markdown of asset price byΔ$\Delta$; in effect, bailout stigma has spread to market sellers int=1$t=1$. Another way to avoid the collateral damage is to accept the bailout but withdraw from thet=2$t=2$market. Indeed, types(θ̂,pg+S]$(\hat{\theta }, p_g+S]$find it strictly profitable to accept the bailout and refuse to sell assets int=2$t=2$to avoid the cost of stigma. The presence of these firms undermines the government's effort to remove the most toxic assets from the market and boost the reputation of the remaining firms. This has a long-term effect, as we turn to next.
  Third, government bailout crowds out private markets in both periods, making the stimulation short-lived. This is caused by the withdrawal of high-type firms from thet=2$t=2$market to avoid stigma, which reduces the reputation of firms thatdoparticipate in thet=2$t=2$market: they are effectively revealed to be of typeθ¯g$\overline{\theta }_g$on average. The high-type firms' behavior leads the stigma to spread to all firms, as noted above, and ends up suppressing market prices int=2$t=2$, including those of firms that did not accept the bailout int=1$t=1$. Naturally, this (anticipated) effect on asset prices feeds back into the asset market int=1$t=1$, dampening its price belowpg$p_g$. This stands in sharp contrast to the static model of Tirole (2012), in which the government offer ofpg$p_g$, which is above the laissez-faire pricep0$p_0$, raises the private market offer abovep0$p_0$to exactlypg$p_g$. In contrast, here the government offer crowds out the markets in both periods, causing them to perform worse than even the laissez-faire benchmark. Indeed, this negative effect is so severe that thet=2$t=2$market freezes to a greater extent than if there were no bailout int=1$t=1$: only typesθ≤θ̂$\theta \le \hat{\theta }$sell int=2$t=2$, where importantlyθ̂<θ0$\hat{\theta }<\theta _0$. By comparison, all typesθ≤θ0$\theta \le \theta _0$would have traded int=2$t=2$in the absence of bailout (recall Figure3). Clearly, transparency leads to a strict loss of trade. Compared with a secret bailout, the volume of trade (and investment) induced under the transparent bailout is the same int=1$t=1$but strictly smaller int=2$t=2$.
  The properties identified so far are necessary but not sufficient for SSE. Specifically, for the existence of SSE, buyers targeting bailout recipients should not gain from raising their offers to attract the boycotters (i.e., typesθ∈[θ̂,pg+S]$\theta \in [\hat{\theta },p_g+S]$), and the buyers targeting nonrecipients should have no incentives to raise their offers to attract holdouts (i.e., typesθ>pg+S$\theta >p_g+S$) together with the market sellers. These conditions are formally stated and shown to be sufficient in SectionII.Aof theInternet Appendix.
  These conditions are not easy to check, so it is difficult to establish the existence of the equilibrium (or its sufficient condition) in a simple manner. Nevertheless, SSE exist for a range of bailout offers under many common distribution functionsF$F$. For example, Panel A of Figure9shows a (continuum of) SSE whenF$F$is uniform.
  However, SSE do not exist ifpg$p_g$is sufficiently high. More precisely, as stated in (iii) of Theorem3, SSE disappear ifpg≥2θ0−E[θ|θ≤θ0]$p_g\ge 2 \theta _0 - \mathbb {E}[ \theta | \theta \le \theta _0]$. Roughly speaking, ifpg$p_g$is sufficiently high, accepting the government offer becomes very attractive, in which case the measure of firms selling to the market int=1$t=1$vanishes. This induces buyers int=2$t=2$market to targett=1$t=1$holdouts with a high offer; anticipating this, int=1$t=1$high-type firms would deviate to hold out instead of selling to the government, undermining the SSE. One implication of this condition is that the policymaker can avoid triggering undesirable SSE by making the bailout offer sufficiently generous—a point that will become clear when we turn to DSE.
  DSE have the structureθ̂≤θ̂g<θ2$\hat{\theta }\le \hat{\theta }_g < \theta _2$in the characterization in Lemma1, as illustrated in Figure8. We call thisdelayed stimulationequilibrium since much of the stimulation effect materializes int=2$t=2$. In particular, the highest type that trades does so int=2$t=2$. Typesθ<θ̂g$\theta <\hat{\theta }_g$act similarly as in SSE: nonnegative fractionsμg$\mu _g$andμm$\mu _m$of typesθ≤θ̂$\theta \le \hat{\theta }$sell to the government and to the market, respectively, and typesθ∈(θ̂,θ̂g)$\theta \in (\hat{\theta }, \hat{\theta }_g)$sell only to the government, whereθ̂≤θ̂g$\hat{\theta }\le \hat{\theta }_g$.
  What makes this equilibrium possible is the incentive thatt=2$t=2$buyers have to offer a sufficiently high price to high-type firms holding out int=1$t=1$. Such an incentive is absent from SSE due to a sizable fractionμm$\mu _m$of low-type market sellers. These firms cannot be distinguished from high-type holdout firms and therefore would inflict a loss to buyers if they were to raise offers to attract high-type holdout firms. In DSE, the fractionμm$\mu _m$of low-type market sellers is sufficiently small, especially whenpg$p_g$is large, thatt=2$t=2$buyers do have an incentive to attract high-type holdouts, unlike in SSE.
  We now provide the characterization of DSE.
  Theorem 4.   Despite these similarities, there is a crucial difference, pertaining to the level ofp2m$p^m_2$. Recall that in SSE, high-type firms accept the bailout int=1$t=1$but withdraw from the market; this means that those firms participating in thet=2$t=2$market tend to have low value, which pushes thet=2$t=2$market prices (bothp2g$p^g_2$andp2m$p^m_2$) down to a level belowpg$p_g$. Hence, as captured by the no-arbitrage equation above and Theorem3, in SSE, bailout stigma spreads to all firms selling in both periods, with the consequence that market prices for all assets fall below the laissez-faire levelp0$p_0$in both periods.
  The high price int=2$t = 2$for the holdout firms incentivizes high-type firms in turn to hold out, validating the premise of the DSE. More interestingly, the high pricep2m=pg$p^m_2=p_g$is not only enjoyed by thet=1$t=1$holdouts but also by thet=1$t=1$market sellers. This feature keeps the bailout stigma from spreading to thet=1$t=1$market sellers. In contrast, their good reputation “propped up” by thet=1$t=1$holdouts mitigates the stigma suffered by the bailout recipients. This can be seen in the no-arbitrage equation above. Substitutingp2m=pg$p^m_2=p_g$in that equation yieldsp2g=pm$p^g_2 = p_m$: bailout recipients enjoy the same price in thet=2$t=2$market as the price that prevails in thet=1$t=1$market. This is why, unlike SSE, the laissez-faire pricep0$p_0$can be supported for both thet=1$t=1$market and thet=2$t=2$market.27In a nutshell, the high-type firms' holdout decision keeps bailout stigma from freezing the markets. Although the government offer does not boost the private market offer as in the static setup of Tirole (2012), it does not suppress the market offer, unlike in SSE.
  The discussions above lead to the following key observation.
  Corollary 1.Each DSE is equivalent to an equilibrium under secret bailout (described in Theorem2) in total volume of asset sales—and thus in total investments undertaken by firms. The total volume of asset sales in any DSE exceeds that in SSE.
  
  
  Proof.See theAppendix.□$\Box$
  
  One interesting, and perhaps surprising, implication of this result is that delays in the effect of a bailout should not be viewed as a policy failure, at least when compared to the secret bailout benchmark. Take a possible equilibrium in whichθ̂g$\hat{\theta }_g$is very low. One can show thatθ̂g=θ0$\hat{\theta }_g=\theta _0$can be supported ifpg$p_g$is not too high (again such an equilibrium arises due to a large mass of high-type firms holding out int=1$t=1$). In such an equilibrium, it may appear att=1$t=1$that bailout has no impact, since trading and investment activity have not changed after the government offer. The impression would be that government purchases “crowd out” private purchases, since a positive measure of firms withθ≤θ0$\theta \le \theta _0$sell to the government at a higher pricepg$p_g$thanp0$p_0$, the price they would have sold at in the absence of bailout. Indeed, in the wake of the Great Recession, such a sentiment prevailed following the apparent lack of response by banks to the first wave of stimulation policies.28
  However, our result suggests that holdout by high-type firms is a blessing in disguise. The presence of these holdout firms is precisely what leads the market to make attractive offers to those that did not accept the bailout. Indistinguishable from these holdout firms, those that actually sold to the market int=1$t=1$also receive attractive offers, thus overcoming the adverse selection endemic to the problem. The alleviation of adverse selection for these firms leads to “collateral benefits” for those that accept the bailout, since no arbitrage means that they can also overcome stigma. Consequently, the increase in trade volume int=2$t=2$more than makes up for the initial lack of response, compared with SSE.
  To an outsider observer, bailouts could very well be seen as working mysteriously here. Paradoxically, the policy invigorates the market by allowing high-type firms to send a strong signal of their financial strength by rejecting the bailout offer. This indirect signaling creates the desired stimulation effect later. As Corollary1suggests, the opportunity to reject a bailout turns out to be more profitable than accepting a bailout.
  To establish the existence of DSE, it is necessary to check that buyers in each period do not have an incentive to deviate from the prescribed equilibrium strategies on every equilibrium path. Just as with SSE, it is difficult to check these “no-buyer-deviation” conditions.29Nevertheless, the equilibrium exists for a sufficiently largepg$p_g$, as stated in Theorem4. For example, the equilibrium always exists under the uniform distribution, as we show in SectionIII.C.
  Corollary1also shows that if apg$p_g$leads to a DSE under the transparent bailout, it yields the same total volume of trade as under a secret bailout. From this, one may conclude that bailout stigma is not of economic concern in a DSE. However, there are two caveats to this conclusion. First, there is a possibility of multiple equilibria, as we show in the next section. That is, the samepg$p_g$may also support an SSE, which is clearly undesirable to a policymaker, as discussed previously. To avoid the selection of such an equilibrium, a policymaker may have to raisepg$p_g$beyond the lower bound identified in Theorem3(iii). This, together with Theorem4, suggests that a delayed equilibrium can be uniquely implemented by an offerpg≥max{2θ0−E[θ|θ≤θ0],E[θ|θ∈[θ0,γ(θ0)]]}$p_g\ge \max \lbrace 2\theta _0 -\mathbb {E}[\theta |\theta \le \theta _0], \mathbb {E}[\theta | \theta \in [\theta _0, \gamma (\theta _0)]]\rbrace$. Second, delay may be undesirable for reasons not modeled in our theory. For instance, prompt revitalization of economic activity often has external benefits for the rest of the economy. In particular, if we consider financial institutions investing in the rest of the economy, prompt restoration of their activities will have a positive spillover effect, and from this perspective delayed stimulation can be harmful. For these reasons, one may view the delay itself as a cost of stigma.
  While it is difficult to find direct evidence on our equilibria, what transpired in the aftermath of the 2007 to 2009 Great Recession is somewhat consistent with our DSE. For instance, firms outright rejected rescue offers made by the government ostensibly to signal their financial strength, as mentioned in the introduction. In particular, our theory suggests that firms with high-quality assets are more likely to hold out when bailout terms are more generous (recall the difference between SSE and DSE). Indeed, there is some evidence that firms with high-quality assets participated less in programs that were more generous. Krishnamurthy, Nagel, and Orlov (2014) find that, during the Great Recession, dealer banks with a large share of agency collateral (i.e., collateral guaranteed by the U.S. government) rarely participated in the PDCF despite their favorable funding rates, although they did participate in the TSLF along with banks with a large share of nonagency collateral (i.e., collateral not guaranteed by the U.S. government).
  Remark 2. (The case of a “full” freeze:θ0=0$\theta _0 = 0$)So far, we implicitly assumeθ0>0$\theta _0>0$for ease of exposition. But our equilibrium characterizations from Theorems3and4extend even to the case ofθ0=0$\theta _0 = 0$, that is, the case in which the market would freeze completely absent any bailout. In this case, our characterizations imply thatθ̂=0$\hat{\theta }=0$, which leads to existence of a unique equilibrium. Typesθ>pg+S$\theta > p_g+S$sell in neither period. One can think of this as a form of DSE: a bailout does not revive the market int=1$t=1$but induces delayed trading int=2$t=2$. article.IV. Cost of Bailouts and the Optimal Bai:
So far we have abstracted from the cost of bailouts, which is nevertheless a crucial element of policy debates. In this section, we evaluate the effect of bailout programs while explicitly accounting for the cost of bailouts. To model the cost, we follow the literature (Tirole (2012), Chiu and Koeppl (2016)) and assume that raising a dollar of public funds to use for the asset purchase program costs society(1+λ)$(1+\lambda)$dollars, whereλ≥0$\lambda \ge 0$represents the deadweight loss of raising public funds, such as distortionary taxation. While a budget deficit entails a social cost, we assume that a budget surplus does not contribute to any direct social welfare. This is in keeping with the primary purpose of government intervention here, which is to bail out distressed firms and to support socially desirable projects, not to profit from the intervention itself.30
  To evaluate the welfare of an equilibrium resulting from government bailouts, we adopt a mechanism design perspective. In so doing, we allow the policymaker to offer a menu of (arbitrarily numerous) bailout programs, not just a single purchase offer, and to adopt any information policy with regard to the identities of the firms that accept the programs. Offering multiple bailout programs is realistic and policy-relevant. During the Great Recession, numerous programs were introduced to help financially distressed firms, which varied in bailout terms and generosity. Similarly, information is an important tool for the policymaker: the policymaker may design the disclosure policy to fine-tune the degree to which the identity of a bailout recipient is revealed to the market.
  While being fully general along these two dimensions, in keeping with our baseline model, we maintain the following assumptions: (a) the government programs are offered only int=1$t=1$; (b) the programs consist of a menuPg$\mathcal {P}_g$of asset purchase offers such thatpg∈[p0,P¯]$p_g \in [p_0, \overline{P}]$for allpg∈Pg$p_g\in \mathcal {P}_g$, for some arbitrarily highP¯$\overline{P}$, so that offered prices are at least as attractive as the laissez-faire price;31and (c) there is no rationing, meaning that any (eligible) firms may choose any program made available by the government.
  Formally, we represent the equilibrium outcome of a bailout policy by a pair of mappings,(Q,T):[0,1]→{0,1,2}×R$(Q, T): [0, 1] \rightarrow \lbrace 0, 1, 2\rbrace \times \mathbb {R}$, whereQ(θ)∈{0,1,2}$Q(\theta)\in \lbrace 0,1,2\rbrace$is a type-θ$\theta$firm's total asset sale across the two periods andT(θ)$T(\theta)$is the total transfer it receives across the two periods in equilibrium. Naturally, there existt=1$t=1$andt=2$t=2$transfer functionst1(θ)$t_1(\theta)$andt2(θ)$t_2(\theta)$, measurable with respect toF(·)$F(\cdot)$, such thatT(θ)=t1(θ)+t2(θ)$T(\theta) = t_1(\theta) + t_2(\theta)$. This transfer includes payment from both the government (if the firm accepts a bailout) and private buyers (if it sells to the market in either period). The fact thatQ$Q$is nonfractional reflects the no-rationing assumption mentioned above. We can view these mappings as adirect mechanismthat implements a social choice as a function of a report on the firm's typeθ$\theta$.
  For the mechanism(Q,T)$(Q, T)$to represent an equilibrium outcome, it must satisfy incentive compatibility and participation constraints. It is worth emphasizing that these constraints are required only for the total two-period allocation and transfers(Q,T)$(Q, T)$. In general, depending on the information the policymaker makes available about the recipients of bailout programs int=1$t=1$, further incentive constraints may be necessary for firms't=2$t=2$payoffs. By ignoring these additional constraints, we focus on the necessary incentive constraints that must hold regardless of the information policy. In this sense, we allow for a fully general information policy.32Given our incentive and participation constraints, one can invoke the celebrated revenue equivalence or envelope theorem to characterize the welfare of a particular equilibrium via the trade volume and payoff for a reference type (e.g., the highest type) induced by the equilibrium. However, the additional constraints (a) and (b) make the standard method inapplicable. The following characterization, which reflects (a) and (b), is crucial for our analysis.

  Part (i) reflects the standard monotonicity condition necessary for incentive compatibility, in the presence of single crossing, as is the case of Lemma1. The fact that the equilibrium trade volume takes a particularly simple form follows from (c), that is, the lack of rationing. The crucial part is thatθ̂≤θ0$\hat{\theta }\le \theta _0$—orQ(θ)≤1$Q(\theta)\le 1$for allθ≥θ0$\theta \ge \theta _0$. This property, which reveals a fundamental limit to the effect of trade stimulation, follows from (a). Lastly, the fact thatθ2≥θ0$\theta _2\ge \theta _0$, which follows from (b), simply means that the government will never gain from suppressing trade below the laissez-faire level.
  Part (ii) shows that the welfare under a general bailout equilibrium consists of three terms. The first term,2θ$2\theta$, is simply the value of a type-θ$\theta$asset; recall that each firm owns two units of such an asset, and its value does not depend on the eventual owner of that asset. The second termS(1+λ)−λF(θ)f(θ)Q(θ)$\left(S(1+\lambda) - \lambda \frac{F(\theta)}{f(\theta)}\right)Q(\theta)$describes the social welfare associated with inducing tradeQ(θ)$Q(\theta)$for a type-θ$\theta$firm. Sale of an asset by a type-θ$\theta$firm yields the social benefit ofS$S$, the return to the project enabled by the sale. Meanwhile, it creates a budget shortfall ofF(θ)f(θ)−S$\frac{F(\theta)}{f(\theta)}-S$, which inflicts a cost ofλ(F(θ)f(θ)−S)$\lambda (\frac{F(\theta)}{f(\theta)}-S)$to society.33Finally, a rent ofu¯−2$\overline{u}-2$accrues to the highest-type (θ=1$\theta =1$) firm (namely, above its asset value 2) and adds to the budget shortfall, inflicting a social cost ofλ(u¯−2)$\lambda (\overline{u}-2)$.34

The resulting trade ruleQ$Q$is still described by (3) withθ̂=θ2=θ0$\hat{\theta }=\theta _2=\theta _0$, and the associated welfare is characterized by (4).35
  Theorem 5.The following characterizes the optimal bailout policy:
  
  
  Proof.See theAppendix.□$\Box$
  
  Theorem5summarizes several broad lessons learned from our general mechanism design analysis. Parts (i) and (ii) of the theorem suggest that the best possible outcome that the government can achieve with its bailout policy can be achieved by asinglebailout offer made in totalsecrecy. A transparent bailout that leads to the DSE can also achieve the same outcome, but the possible multiplicity of equilibria complicates the problem, as we discuss below.
  First, the optimality of total secrecy means that even if the government can design its information policy with regard to firms' acceptance of the offers without any constraints, it can never do strictly better than keeping the market in the dark about the identities of the bailout recipients. This follows from the fact that the information revelation and separation of types exacerbate adverse selection and market unraveling (recall footnote). In light of Corollary1, this means that the optimum can be attained by the DSE under transparency, which is important in practice given the difficulty of maintaining secrecy. In particular, this implies that the SSE is strictly worse, as stated in part (iii). Note that this conclusion is not a priori obvious from Corollary1, since for any DSE associated withpg$p_g$, an SSE with some suitably chosenpg′$p_g^{\prime }$may implement the same total volume of trade. What part (iii) suggests is that even when such an offer exists, it would require a strictly higher deficit on the part of the government and therefore a strictly higher social cost. Intuitively, a more severe stigma arising from that equilibrium requires a larger deficit to support the same level of stimulation. Admittedly, the bailout offerpg$p_g$that implements the optimal outcome under a DSE may also admit a less desirable SSE. To avoid this situation, the policymaker may need to raise the offer further to a level for which the latter type of equilibrium is no longer possible, although this may not implement the optimal policy. This result is also consistent with the Fed's decision to lower the interest rate charged to the banks borrowing from the DW in the early phase of the Great Recession.
  The second implication of part (ii) is that it does not pay policymakers to offer multiple bailout programs. Indeed, part (iv) shows that doing so under full transparency is strictly suboptimal inanyequilibrium in which firms self-select into multiple programs. This finding does not necessarily contradict the approach taken by the U.S. government in the wake of the Great Recession. The plethora of bailout programs rolled out by the government targeted different segments of the financial market and the broader economy, whereas part (iv) concerns the multiplicity of programs targeting thesamesegment of the economy. One example of the latter case is the coexistence of the DW and the TAF, both of which targeted depository institutions. Our result suggests that the Fed could have improved the efficacy of the public liquidity provision if it had chosen either DW (at a very low borrowing rate) or TAF alone, not both. Running both programs could have worsened the overall perception of not only the banks using the DW facility, but also the TAF users and the banks that did not participate in either program. Put differently, it is never desirable to offer a menu of multiple programs solely for the purpose of eliciting firms' types. Doing so backfires by compounding the incentive constraints. The multiplicity of programs leads to more separation of firm types, which exacerbates adverse selection and market freeze.
article.V. Related Literature:
While the broad theme of this paper is related to an extensive literature on the benefits and costs of government intervention in distressed banks,37our work is most closely related to Philippon and Skreta (2012) and Tirole (2012), who focus on adverse selection in asset markets as a primary reason for government intervention.38As mentioned previously, these papers do not explicitly study the dynamic consequence of receiving a bailout—the focus of the current study. Even though these papers recognize that relatively low types accept bailouts, this does not translate into an adverse effect on subsequent financing in their models. Our dynamic model captures not only how bailout stigma affects firms' financing behavior, but also how stigma fundamentally alters the role of a bailout. In particular, our most striking takeaway is the ability of firms to favorably signal their type by rejecting an attractive bailout offer, which has no analog in these or other previous studies.
  Banks' reputational concerns are explicitly considered in Ennis and Weinberg (2013), La'O (2014), Chari, Shourideh, and Zetlin-Jones (2014), Ennis (2019), and Hu and Zhang (2023). In Ennis and Weinberg (2013), to meet their short-term liquidity needs, banks with high-quality assets use interbank lending while those with low-quality assets use the DW. The resulting discount window stigma is reflected in the subsequent pricing of assets. In La'O (2014), financially strong banks use the Federal Reserve's TAF since winning the auction at a premium signals financial strength, which protects them from predatory trading. Hu and Zhang (2023) study banks' choice between the DW and TAF where funds are immediately available from the DW, albeit entailing a stigma, whereas TAF releases funds with delay. They show that banks self-select themselves into different programs. The main focus in Chari, Shourideh, and Zetlin-Jones (2014) is on how reputational concerns in secondary loan markets can result in persistent adverse selection. Since all three studies consider discrete types of banks and there is no government bailout, their results are not directly comparable to ours. Ennis (2019) extends Philippon and Skreta (2012) by allowing banks to borrow from the DW before borrowing from the market, thereby formalizing the DW stigma alluded to in Philippon and Skreta (2012). There are two main differences between Ennis (2019) and our work. First, there is only one investment opportunity in Ennis (2019), which does not capture the dynamics of a stimulation effect (e.g., the possibility of stimulation being either short-lived or delayed). Second, Ennis (2019) characterizes equilibria for exogenously given DW rates, whereas we study the optimal bailout policy.
  Our paper is also related to studies on dynamic adverse selection in general (Inderst and Müller (2002), Janssen and Roy (2002), Moreno and Wooders (2010), Camargo and Lester (2014), Fuchs and Skrzypacz (2015)) and those with a focus on the role of information in particular (Hörner and Vieille (2009), Daley and Green (2012), Fuchs, Öry, and Skrzypacz (2016), Kim (2017)).39The key insight from the first set of studies is that dynamic trading generates sorting opportunities, which are not available in the static market setting. However, each seller has only one opportunity to trade in these studies, so signaling is not an issue. The second set of studies relates to different disclosure rules and how they affect dynamic trading. For example, Hörner and Vieille (2009) and Fuchs, Öry, and Skrzypacz (2016) show that secrecy (private offers) tends to alleviate adverse selection but transparency (public offers) does not. Once again, each seller has only one trading opportunity in these studies. Thus, while past rejections can boost reputation, acceptance ends the game. In contrast, in our model, acceptance as well as rejection of the bailout offer work as signaling opportunities. Although our model also shows that secret bailouts weakly dominate transparent bailouts, none of these papers studies government intervention in response to market failure.
  Several empirical studies provide evidence on stigma in the financial market. Peristiani (1998) provides early evidence on the DW stigma. Furfine (2001,2003) finds similar evidence from the Federal Reserve's Special Lending Facility during the 1999 to 2000 period and the new DW facility introduced in 2003. Armantier et al. (2015) utilize the Federal Reserve's TAF bid data from the 2007 to 2008 financial crisis to estimate the cost of stigma and its effect. Cassola, Hortaçsu, and Kastl (2013) find evidence of stigma from the bidding data from the European Central Bank's auctions of one-week loans. Krishnamurthy, Nagel, and Orlov (2014) find that in repo financing, dealer banks with higher shares of agency collateral repayments, that is, collateral (implicitly) guaranteed by the government, borrowed less from the PDCF despite its attractive funding terms, which supports the existence of a stigma attached to the users of the PDCF.
  Finally, a vast literature examines the Great Recession. The Congressional Budget Office (2012) estimates the overall cost of the TARP at approximately $32 billion, the largest part of which stems from assistance to American International Group, Inc. (AIG) and the automotive industry while capital injections to financial institutions are estimated to have yielded a net gain. For detailed assessments of the various programs in the TARP, see Moretti (2015). See also Fleming (2012), who discusses how the various emergency liquidity facilities provided by the Federal Reserve during the 2007 to 2009 crisis were designed to overcome the limitations of traditional policy instruments at a time of crisis. Tong and Wei (2020) provide international evidence on the effect of unconventional interventions during 2008 to 2010.
article.VI. Conclusion:
In the current paper, we study a dynamic model of a government bailout in which firms have a continuing need to fund their projects by selling their assets. Asymmetric information about the quality of assets gives rise to adverse selection and a concomitant market freeze, which provides a rationale for a government bailout, as in Tirole (2012). In contrast to Tirole (2012), however, markets stigmatize bailout recipients, which jeopardizes their ability to fund subsequent projects. The presence of this bailout stigma and other dynamic incentives yields a much more complex and nuanced portrayal of how bailouts impact the economy than has been recognized in extant literature.
  Our main findings can be summarized as follows. Bailout stigma requires that the government pay a premium over the market to compensate for the stigma. Even so, market rejuvenation can be short-lived and adverse selection can become more pronounced in subsequent market trading, resulting in a market freeze that is even more severe than in the absence of a bailout. This induces the government to increase the premium further. A more attractive bailout premium can stimulate trade and investment, but its effects are delayed. Delayed benefits arise as bailouts provide firms with opportunities to boost reputation by “rejecting” bailout offers, which improves their ability to trade in the market in subsequent periods. Indeed, there is no welfare loss in this case relative to a secret bailout, which maximizes welfare. As such, the delayed effects of bailouts can be a blessing in disguise, subject to two important caveats: the government may need to run a large budget deficit to support delayed market stimulation, and delay in and of itself may be undesirable for reasons not modeled in the current paper.
  The central lesson from this study is that, compared with the static setting, the dynamic effects of bailouts are very different due to the interactions between bailout stigma, market beliefs within and across periods, and firms' rich signaling opportunities in the dynamic context. To the best of our knowledge, the insights we develop and the forces we identify are novel and have not been recognized in previous literature, and should be part of the framework in future policy debates and empirical studies.
